All right, you're back and it's time for your bonus lesson on prospecting, which is big picture questions. You're going to love this. So what are big picture questions? Well, simply put, big picture questions are exactly what they sound like. They're general, non-invasive questions that have been designed to establish a frame, a general frame for a specific line of questioning that follows. Let me repeat that. Big picture questions are general, non-invasive questions that are designed to establish an overarching or general frame for a set of questions, a specific line of questions that following. In other words, you might ask a big picture question that will then set you up to ask a series of other questions that dig more deeply into a certain topic. You get it? So let me put up the seven most common big picture questions right now. Essentially, these are the ones that apply to most industries as you gather intelligence. Now again, whether or not they apply to your industry, well, that depends, but don't worry about it. When we go through our exercises after your quiz, we'll go through these one by one and through process of elimination, I'll show you exactly which ones apply to your industry. So let me quickly take you through these right now one by one, starting with number one. What do you like and for that matter, dislike with the current product or service you're using? Again, I'm not going to explain these right now. It's going to go through them. We'll do this after. Next, what would you change or improve with your current source? Number three, what would be your ideal program if you could totally design it for yourself? Next, what's your biggest headache right now in terms of what's really keeping you up at night? It's a pain in the ass, right? Next, what is your ultimate objective here in terms of what are you really looking to accomplish at the highest level? Next, of all the facts we've talked about, which is the most important to you of all? And then lastly, have I asked you about everything that's important to you? Have I exhausted everything here? Great question, right? So again, those are your big picture questions and their primary function is to establish a general frame from which you're going to then follow up by asking a series of industry specific questions, which you've already been identifying in the previous exercises in this module, right? And of course, in line with all the other rules of straight line prospecting, you'll always be following the logical path as you ask those questions. And we get to the scripting module, by the way, you'll see how we then merge these two types of questions together, your big picture questions and your industry specific questions to create this near perfect intelligence gathering session, which then sets you up for massive success throughout the rest of the sale. Make sense? Okay, great. Now, again, as I said before, we'll go through which of these big picture questions directly applies to your industry during the exercises. But for right now, there are three of these questions in particular that can be staggeringly effective when they use the proper sequence. Let me take you through those right now specific strategy, starting with the basic premise of it, which is that regardless of what product or service you're selling, in the vast majority of cases, this is not going to be the first time the prospect is using your type of solution to fill this need. In other words, they use similar products in the past with similar features, similar benefits, their own cost benefit ratios, their own relative value proposition, you get it? Now, obviously, your particular product has certain key points of difference, which you'll bring it to higher relief during your sales presentation, right? That's what you're going to do. But rather than trying to guess which of these points of difference are most valuable to your prospect, with this particular strategy, we're going to get them to tell us so. Now, the only problem with that is that it's awesome as all this sounds in theory. And in fact, as awesome as it plays out in the real world when it's executed correctly, the operative word here is correctly. For example, you simply ask that question upfront without first building the proper foundation beneath it to the conclusion of break for poor, which is pretty much the last thing you want to do at this point is runs totally counter to those two crucial outcomes of straight line prospecting, which are to simultaneously gather intelligence and build rapport, right? Because that's what with this particular strategy, we ask a set of three big picture questions in a certain predetermined sequence, thereby ensuring those dual outcomes. And your first question is going to be, so tell me, John, what do you like about the current product using? That's number one. Now, remember, like I said before, they're using almost certainly a product similar to yours. You know, other cars in the past, if you're a car salesman, they've dealt with other brokers, bought other vacuum cleaners, lawn mowers, dealt with other dog trainers, dog rumors, you get it? So that's why you're going to ask, so tell me, John, what did you like about the last product slash person that you dealt with? But what are the features about that you really like most? Now, obviously, the reason you're doing this is not to make the other product look good and hire how great theirs is. It's because we'll perfectly set the stage for your next question in the sequence, which is, and now tell me, John, what didn't you like about it? What things would you change with your current source, or current broker or person? See, if you just come at them with the second question first, they'll be like, oh, yeah, I see what you're doing. You're trying to shop shoot me. And you just come as being a bit too slick. But by doing the mirror images of each other, the so-called yin and then the yang, well, then it makes perfect sense to them, and they'll tell you everything, what they liked, what they didn't like, what they would change, what features they'd expand on, what drove them crazy about the last product, what they considered a waste of their time or money. And then from there, on this information, the next logical follow-up question is going to be, and tell me, John, if you could design this product for yourself, you pick all the features and benefits, what would that product look like? I really want to know. And just like that, they'll really draw you a detailed roadmap as to exactly what they need to hear from you right now about your product or service in order to close, which is to say they've just hand you the keys to the kingdom. You get it? Powerful stuff. Now, in addition to that, another one of these big picture questions you can ask would be, have I asked you about everything that's important to you? Anything I left out? Anything you want to add? Now think about that. When you go to a doctor, if that sounds strange, what does a great doctor do? One of the great bedside manner. They'll always say to you at the end, anything I left out? Anything you want to add? Anything you want to tell me? That's what a great physician, a great diagnostician does because they don't want to get anything wrong. That's what all experts do. So there's more big picture questions back in your exercise area, right? So go through them all, pick out the ones that fit what you're doing, and you're going to pepper them in with the rest of your specific questions you have to ask. One point though, make sure you ask those three in sequence and watch what happens. So with that, it's time to go to our quiz. I'm going to make this a short lesson here, right? And after you've done with your quiz, make sure that you spend some time going through the exercises here because in these exercises, they're going to have you actually map out your three big picture questions, those three I talked about, and also we'll go through the others as well to make sure you understand them at a basic level. All good? All right? After you're done, that will end this module and we'll be rocking on to the next one. Things are really looking up, guys. We're doing great. I'll see you soon.
